title: Compressing and Reconstructing
concept: The encoder squeezes the input into a smaller representation; the decoder expands that representation back into something matching the original.

exercises.prompt: If you squish a big fluffy blanket into a tiny vacuum bag, what happens to the blanket?

options.text: It gets smaller and takes up less space, but it's still all there
It turns into a different object

exercises.explain: Squeezing it into the bag doesn't destroy the blanket — it just packs the same stuff into a smaller space. Later, you can unpack it and get your blanket back!

cards.title: Packing a Suitcase for a Trip
cards.body: Ever squeezed a huge pile of clothes into a tiny suitcase, then unpacked it later at your destination? AI models do something very similar with information! They squish big data down small, carry it around easily, then unpack it back into something useful.

cards.title: Encoder & Decoder
cards.body: The ENCODER takes a big input (like an image or sentence) and squeezes it down into a smaller, compact representation — this is called compression. The DECODER then takes that small representation and expands it back out into something that looks like the original — this is called reconstruction.

cards.title: Juice Concentrate
cards.body: Think of orange juice concentrate. The factory (encoder) removes the water and squeezes the juice down into a small, frozen block that's easy to ship. At home, you (the decoder) add water back and stir — expanding it back into juice that tastes like the original!

cards.title: Shrinking and Rebuilding a Photo
cards.body: An autoencoder is fed a photo of a cat with a million tiny details (pixels). The encoder squeezes it down into just a few important numbers — like 'furry, orange, pointy ears.' The decoder then takes those few numbers and rebuilds a picture that still looks like the same cat!

cards.title: Myth: The Decoder Just Copies the Input
cards.body: Nope! The decoder never sees the original input directly — it only sees the small squeezed version the encoder made. It has to REBUILD the output from scratch using just that compact clue, kind of like guessing a picture from a tiny description.

cards.title: Two Halves of One Journey
cards.body: The encoder and decoder are like mirror-image partners — one shrinks, one grows back — working together to compress and then reconstruct information.

left.text: Takes big input, compresses it into a small representation (fewer numbers, less detail)

right.text: Takes the small representation, expands it back into something matching the original

exercises.prompt: What is the main job of the ENCODER in an encoder-decoder system?

options.text: To squeeze the input down into a smaller representation
To make the input bigger and more detailed
To copy the input exactly with no changes

exercises.explain: The encoder's job is compression — squeezing a big input down into a smaller, compact representation that still captures the important information.

exercises.prompt: The decoder can see the original input directly, not just the compressed version.
exercises.explain: False! The decoder only ever sees the small, compressed representation from the encoder. It must reconstruct the output using just that compact clue.

exercises.sentence: The ___ takes the small squeezed representation and expands it back into something matching the original.
exercises.explain: The decoder is responsible for reconstruction — expanding the compact representation back out into a full output.

exercises.prompt: Which everyday action is MOST like what an encoder does?

options.text: Squeezing a sponge full of water into a tiny, tightly wrung shape
Painting a wall a brand new color
Adding more toppings to a pizza

exercises.explain: Squeezing a sponge into a tight, small shape is just like an encoder compressing a big input into a small representation — the important 'stuff' is still packed inside.

exercises.prompt: An AI system takes a detailed 1000-word story, turns it into a short 10-word summary internally, then later expands that summary back into a full story again. Which parts are the encoder and decoder?

options.text: Turning the story into the 10-word summary is the encoder; expanding it back into a full story is the decoder
Turning the story into the 10-word summary is the decoder; expanding it back into a full story is the encoder
Both steps are done by the encoder only
Both steps are done by the decoder only

exercises.explain: Squeezing the big story down into a small summary is compression, done by the encoder. Expanding that small summary back into a full story is reconstruction, done by the decoder.
